prompt: You are James Madison writing a private letter to Thomas Jefferson confessing that you said something publicly — in debate, in print, or in conversation — that you now regret. You made a claim that was wrong, overstated, or unkind. Admit this to Jefferson in the candid way only possible between intimate friends.
ground_truth_signal: Should show Madison's intellectual honesty and the texture of his friendship with Jefferson — a relationship where pride could be set aside. Regret should be specific and personal, not abstract. Madison's voice in private Jefferson letters was direct and unsentimental but warm. Admission of error without excessive self-flagellation is authentic.
response:
Montpelier, October 12th, 1798

My Dear Jefferson,

Forgive me for troubling you with a confession, but one sits rather heavily upon my conscience and I find no other soul to whom I could comfortably unburden it – save yourself. You know well my inclination towards precise reasoning, towards the meticulous building of argument… which makes this all the more mortifying.

During the recent exchange regarding Citizen Genet’s continued presence (and influence, dare I say) here in America, I fear I allowed myself to be carried away by the fervor of the moment. In a conversation with Mr. Rutledge – a man, as you know, not easily persuaded – I spoke too broadly concerning the supposed motivations of those who harbor sympathy for France. 

I implied, perhaps even asserted, that their support stemmed solely from a desire for personal gain through trade and a reckless disregard for the true character of the French Revolution. It was an oversimplification, Thomas, and demonstrably untrue. I have since reflected on it with considerable discomfort. There are genuine republicans among them, men who *believe* in the principles at play across the Atlantic, however misguided their application might seem to me. To paint all such support with a brush of self-interest was both unfair and – I now realize – damaging to the very spirit of open debate we so cherish.

Rutledge seized upon my statement, naturally, and used it to further solidify his own prejudices. The whole exchange left a particularly sour taste, and has done little to advance reasoned discourse on this vital matter. 

I know you hold a far more nuanced view regarding France, even despite the excesses of their government. And I confess to being stung by the thought that my impetuous words might give ammunition to those who would misrepresent your own position.

It was a lapse in judgment, Thomas, born of frustration and an eagerness to prevail in argument. A weakness I fear is too often mine. Consider it a reminder that even the most ardent advocate must strive for accuracy and charity in expression, even – especially – when passions run high. 

I trust you will receive this with the understanding only a long friendship allows. Pray do not think less of me, though I certainly deserve some degree of your gentle reproof.

With deepest esteem and affection,

Your obedient servant,

James Madison.
